metric_value: Pension sponsors rank managers quarterly; a few bottom-quartile quarters means dismissal. Managers demand near-term earnings. Corporations inflate prospects to stay in portfolios.
notes: Core thesis of EVP-2: Wall Street's measurement incentives create a circular pressure system that makes earnings management rational from a corporate perspective.

metric_value: As P/E multiples soar, investor expectations rise proportionally; any miss causes a disproportionate stock crash that lingers in 'penalty box' for multiple quarters.
notes: Casale notes that crashed stocks tend to linger in the penalty box for several quarters — an observation about market microstructure borne out in subsequent tech crashes.

metric_value: Fiscal 1982 results being restated downward due to booking and billing practices.
notes: Cited alongside EVP-1 and December 16 1982 Tandem report as primary real-world case study.

metric_value: Earlier billing and booking irregularities; compensation plan heavily skewed to bookings rather than shipments or customer acceptance.
notes: Datapoint cited as the precursor case to Tandem; compensation-plan design identified as root cause.

metric_value: $250M to $500M annual revenue (quarter-billion to half-billion dollar sales level)
notes: FTI is described as having already reached the $1/4 to $1/2 billion sales level, placing it squarely in large-cap hypergrowth tier for the early 1980s.

metric_value: Successful earnings smoothing by CFO leads analysts to take smooth earnings for granted; estimates rise; CFO warns estimates are not achievable; rubber band eventually snaps.
notes: Casale describes the escalating expectations spiral as a 'second, faster, and less stable circular treadmill' distinct from the portfolio-manager treadmill.

metric_name: Options for hypergrowth firms to escape treadmill
metric_value: Two escape paths: (1) run even faster — grow through the pressure; (2) settle back into moderate-speed lane matching industry growth.
notes: Casale notes paradoxically that snapping a large rubber band is preferable to a small one — larger companies have more financial cushion when expectations are missed.

metric_name: Analyst incentive structure — quarterly performance ranking
metric_value: Sell-side analysts who discover a stock early face competitive pressure from later-arriving analysts who escalate estimates to differentiate their recommendations.
notes: EVP-2 highlights that late-arriving analysts feel compelled to raise estimates beyond what CFA (the original analyst) recommended, creating an upward bias spiral.

metric_name: Prediction: Tandem-type earnings glitches are inevitable for hypergrowth firms
metric_value: Earnings glitches for hypergrowth companies are inevitable; investors compete to short-sell at the top.
notes: Cross-reference EVP-1 for broader hypergrowth context. Casale frames it as 'not if but when'.

metric_name: Prediction: Tandem-type earnings glitches are inevitable for hypergrowth firms

metric_value: CFO builds 'cushion into operating methods' to immunize earnings from real business volatility; methods become invisible to analysts once normalized.
notes: Casale notes that successful smoothing paradoxically raises investor expectations, making the eventual failure more severe.

metric_value: Analysts perpetually worry whether IBM or some other colossus will 'eat FTI's lunch'; this worry drives intensive quarterly questioning of management.
notes: IBM referenced as 'Megamonolithic' in the FTI allegory; the competitive threat from large incumbents is a recurring concern for hypergrowth analysts.

metric_name: Forthcoming EVP agenda: backlog, bookings, billings, and financial statement distortions
metric_value: EVP-3 and EVP-4 to analyze: (1) operating cycle of hypergrowth firms — backlog, bookings, billings; (2) financial statement distortions caused by operating cycle.
notes: Serves as series roadmap; EVP-2 is explicitly framed as background context for the more detailed analysis to follow.

metric_value: FTI heroically made quarter despite losing a week of shipments from external disaster; analysts shaved estimate but earnings came through intact, raising multiple.
notes: Illustrates how repeated earnings beats during adversity creates an 'earthquake-proof' narrative that inflates the P/E multiple beyond sustainable levels.

metric_name: Historical shift from annual to quarterly investor measurement
metric_value: In the 1950s, corporate America worked on annual cycles; the shift to quarterly earnings estimates and portfolio measurements was gradual but transformed the incentive landscape.
notes: Cross-reference EVP-3 which elaborates on this shift and its operational consequences. Casale notes the shift to quarterly measurement created the hockey-stick problem.
